AI tool discovery audit

Athena pain-point review

A clean, browsable version of the Obsidian audit notes plus the Jun 19 Google Docs meeting test. The structure keeps the original Chatbot, Agent, and Whiteboard audit, then adds the collaboration tools and AI apps tested in the follow-up session.

Source notes

Athena AI Tool Discovery
→ Obsidian audit notes
→ Jun 19 Google Docs Notes + Transcript

Tip: click any screenshot to view it larger.

01

Athena Chatbot

Chat, media generation, and editor controls

The chatbot audit focuses on chat usability, missing conversation management, and rough edges in video, music, and image-generation editing flows.

General

  • After prompting Athena again in the same chat, it does not autoscroll to the bottom of the chat.
  • Chats cannot be deleted.

Video generation tool

Video editor

  • Cannot use CTRL-Z to undo changes inside the timeline.
  • Cannot physically select clips after splitting them.

30-second music clip

  • Only the initial generation relates to the generated video; prompt-based changes are not available after generation.
  • Cannot choose preferences for song output, such as tone or genre, so results feel seemingly random.

Image generation

Image editor

  • Sliders are too sensitive: tiny values such as 0.5 saturation or -0.5 brightness create a large shift in the photo.
  • Manual text boxes help work around slider sensitivity, but they only accept integers and do not support float values.
02

Athena Agent

Agent setup, output clarity, and recovery states

The source Agent note branches into two pages: Chat Agent and Autopilot Agent. Together they surface onboarding complexity, missing templates, unclear file locations, and broken manual-intervention recovery.

Same structure as the note: Athena Agent is a parent section with two linked audits underneath it: Athena Chat Agent and Athena Autopilot Agent.

Athena Chat Agent — Agent Creation

  • Basic fields like name and description and advanced fields like knowledge overrides and MCP options are shown in one flow, making first-time setup feel overwhelming.
  • Some options cannot be configured until the agent is saved and re-loaded. A categorized flow could save automatically after each phase.
  • A phased flow would separate basic setup fields such as name, description, and prompt, then move to the next phase after clicking Continue or Next.
  • There is no guided setup wizard or pre-made templates for common chatbot types.

Athena Chat Agent — Agent Usage

  • The bot initially included things at the end of responses that did not make sense.Example: it pulled information from an example client email and repeated the signature at the end of every prompt response.
  • The bot invented website observations even when no website was provided.
  • The tone was good, but extra instructions were needed to avoid false claims and to use placeholders for missing details.

Athena Autopilot Agent — Agent Usage

  • The agent wrote an audit file but did not clearly state where to find it, only saying it was under the current session directory.
  • Asking where that directory was caused an infinite “Thinking” loading bar.
  • After manually stopping the loading bar, the agent finally returned a result.
  • The setup requested a soft business-website audit, not the deep technical dive that was produced.
  • The setup requested a well-written owner email explaining how the website and lead conversion could be improved, but the audit report file did not include that email.
  • Manual intervention is unclear: there are no obvious buttons for cancel, back, reload, or next action.Observed workaround: close the browser, quit the app, and re-launch. This is not ideal.
03

Athena Whiteboard

Layer control, selection, erasing, and board interpretation

The whiteboard findings are mostly about precision and predictability: locking, layers, selecting the right item, erasing safely, and preserving board state across themes.

Usage

  • There is no way to lock a layer and work above it.
  • There is no layer list for quickly selecting the right element; selection requires fiddling manually with the cursor.
  • The eraser tool erases everything it touches, making it hard to erase the intended object.
  • When switching between light and dark modes, chess pieces switch colours too, which should not happen.
  • Attaching a chess-board snapshot and asking the chat for the next best move produced an unhelpful response.
04

Jun 19 meeting tool test

Collaboration tools, permissions, and AI app coverage

The Google Docs auto-notes and transcript were rewritten into the same audit language as the original site: direct findings, no meeting narration, and no participant-by-participant recap.

Source treatment: The meeting Notes tab was used as the main source, with the Transcript tab checked for exact context. The findings below remove conversational wording and keep only product behavior, bugs, and testing scope decisions.

Meeting notes and attendance

  • Row synchronization worked in the current test, but prior stale-session behavior can make rows appear missing between users.
  • Any participant can delete rows created by another participant. Deletion should be permission-based, while still allowing each participant to edit their own rows.
  • The Hide AI chat control is too far from the chat surface. If the action controls the chat, it should live inside the chat interface.
  • Save Meeting exports the final meeting state as a link, but the label does not clearly explain the result.

Collaborative whiteboard

  • The host-only green share/collaboration button can block whiteboard UI elements and creates a different layout between host and collaborator.
  • There is no obvious full-screen toggle for the whiteboard.
  • Collaboration can drop after a connection issue, leaving the collaborator unable to keep working on the board.
  • Grouping and selecting multiple whiteboard items worked during the test.

Permissions and Google Drive

  • Save to Google Drive and Open from Google Drive lock into one Google account without a reliable account-switching path.
  • The Drive integration becomes unusable when the wrong account is active or multiple Google accounts are signed in.
  • The Drive account should be tied consistently to the account used in the meeting session; manual account switching should not create undefined behavior.
  • Read-only/edit permission changes do not apply in real time. Users may need to leave and rejoin before the updated access level takes effect.
  • When editing is blocked, the interface should explain why instead of failing silently.

Groups and chat history

  • The chat box does not scroll reliably.
  • Default avatar images can appear broken.
  • Private messages and group chats are not separated clearly enough in the history list.

Live image editor

  • The contrast slider feels sluggish.
  • The activity can crash or force the browser to refresh.
  • The editor needs more stability testing before it can be treated as reliable.

Polls and questions

  • The tool appears limited to one poll at a time.
  • Retaking a poll or adding another poll is not straightforward for participants.

Collaborative notes

  • Tables, headers, and images insert at the bottom of the document instead of the current cursor location.
  • Toolbar behavior is inconsistent: some actions have hover descriptions, but many buttons do not explain what they do.
  • Inserted links can be rewritten with extra application path text instead of opening the intended URL.
  • Button descriptions/actions should be documented so the toolbar is easier to audit and QA.

Timer and agenda

  • Timer values can differ between users.
  • Resume/restart actions do not reliably update for everyone in the activity.

AI apps and recommenders

  • Subject-specific AI whiteboards appear to reuse the same base whiteboard app with different pre-filled prompts, so each variation does not need a separate deep test pass.
  • The AI chat can generate diagram-style outputs such as class diagrams, Gantt charts, and flowcharts.
  • Whiteboard snapshots can be attached to the AI chat and used as context for modifying the board content.
  • Mail Magic AI fails to load.
  • Travel and AliExpress recommenders use the same Athena chat interface; collaboration works, but recommendation outputs can drift into generated website-style responses.
Built as a standalone static page from the local Obsidian notes repository and the Jun 19 Google Docs meeting notes. No remote assets are required.
